ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 11 March 2013 Received in revised form 16 February 2014 Accepted 28 February 2014|This paper explores the carbon footprint of milk produced by dairy cows in Australia and follows the common carbon footprint approach guidelines of the International Dairy Federation and the Interna- tional Standard for Life Cycle Assessment. Primary data were collected and analysed from 139 farms representing the main dairy regions in Australia. The scope of the study covers all inputs and outputs from cradle to farm gate for the twelve month period from July 2009 to June 2010 (2009/2010). The carbon footprint of average Australian milk for 2009/2010 was calculated to be 1.11 kg carbon dioxide equivalents kg-1 fat and protein corrected milk. Differences in GHG emission per kg of milk produced could be traced back to differences in production practices. The feed conversion efficiency was identified as the most important driver for the reduction of enteric methane emissions. Reduction potentials were also identified for manure management practices. 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
2: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2007) has concluded that the agricultural sector contributes 10-12% to overall global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The dairy industry is responding to an increased national and international demand for information regarding the environmental impact of products, especially with regard to greenhouse gas emissions. Concerns over the role of livestock, in particular the dairy sector's contribution to climate change, have led to numerous scientific studies aimed at improving the scientific knowledge regarding the sector's emis- sions, as well as harmonising inventory approaches. Life cycle assessment (LCA) studies have been conducted on the global level (FAO, 2010; IDF, 2009; Sevenster & Jong, 2008) and at a national level (including DairyCo, 2012; Lundie et al., 2002, Lundie, Schulz, Peters, Nebel, & Ledgard, 2009; Thoma et al., 2012) to quantify the environmental impacts associated with dairy milk production. Numerous studies have also been performed on the comprehensive methodologies associated with life cycle assessment in the dairy sector (Flysjö, Henrikson, Cederberg, Ledgard & Englund, 2011;

Flysjö, Henriksson, Cederberg, & Ledgard, 2012). Following these developments the dairy sector has developed industry specific carbon footprint guidelines (IDF, 2010) to provide a consistent methodology specific to conducting carbon footprints for the dairy sector (IDF, 2010).

Australian dairy farms annually produce around 9 billion litres of raw milk of which about 38% is exported as dairy products (DA, 2010b). Imports account for less than 200,000 tonnes of dairy products. Most of the Australian dairy production occurs in the southern part of the country where the climate and natural re- sources are favourable. Dairy farming practices in these regions rely predominantly on pasture-based feed systems with feedlot (or housing based systems) making up only a minor part of the in- dustry (DA, 2010a).

Between 2010 and 2012, the Australian dairy sector conducted a greenhouse gas life cycle assessment study of all major dairy products. The focus of this paper is the production of milk from the cradle to farm gate. The goals of this study were to quantify the carbon footprint of average dairy milk production in Australia (production weighted) and to establish a software platform that could be used for this and future studies. This paper focuses on the first goal of the study (carbon footprint). Additional information on the project can be found on the Dairy Australia website (DA, 2010b).

5: This study followed the International Dairy Federation (IDF) carbon footprint guidelines: "A common carbon footprint approach for dairy" (IDF, 2010) and the life cycle assessment standards ISO 14040 and 14044 (ISO, 2006a,b). The specific details of the methods used are outlined in the following sub-sections.
6: <header>2.1. Functional unit, time period covered and impact assessment </header>

The functional unit of the study was 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) at the farm gate as per the IDF guidelines (IDF, 2010). Standardised milk fat and protein content (4% fat and 3.3% protein) enables fair comparisons between different breeds and feed regimes. Inventory data were collected for the 2009/2010 financial year and therefore results are also representative for this period. The national average rainfall for this period was 8% higher than the 30 year average (1960-1990) and the mean temperature was 0.9 °C above the 30 year average (ABS, 2011).

Current IPCC 100 year global warming potential (GWP) char- acterisation factors were applied to convert greenhouse gas emis- sions into carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2 e) emissions. The two common factors used were: 1 kg methane (CH4) = 25 kg CO2 e and 1 kg of nitrous oxide (N20) = 298 kg CO2 e (IPCC, 2007).
7: The system boundary contained all relevant activities from cradle to farm gate including the on-farm activities (foreground system) and the production of farm inputs (background system) as indicated in Fig. 1. The activities within the foreground system include those that are under direct control of the farmer that are related to dairy production such as: animal husbandry, on-farm feed production, pasture management, manure management, fuel combustion and contractors. It also includes livestock agisted to other farms. The inventory for the foreground system was based on primary data collected at the individual farm level. The background system contains all relevant inputs to the foreground system including: off-site feed production; production of syn- thetic fertilisers, fuels, and electricity production; transport of
fodder, fertilisers and fuels to the farm; and water supply. The background system was modelled based on publicly available data.
8: In addition to milk, all dairy farms inevitably produce additional products including surplus calves, culled dairy cows and other cattle that leave the farm (co-products of milk production). The method used to divide the carbon footprint between these different products is called allocation. Technically speaking, allocation refers to partitioning the environmental impact of a multifunctional process or product system between the product system under study and one or more other product systems (ISO, 2006b). Calves not required to replace culled dairy cows usually leave the farm at a young age. In some cases they are sold later as heifers or bulls, or used to grow the milking herd. In accordance with ISO (2006b) and IDF (2010) a physical allocation method was used to partition the environmental impacts associated with each co-product. This approach was based on the physiological feed requirement of the animal to produce "milk and meat". The allocation factor (AF) is calculated, for each individual farm, based on the following equa- tion (IDF, 2010): AF = 1 - 5.7717*R

where R = kg livestock output farm kg 1 FPCM output farm The livestock output was on average 17.6 g (live weight) per kilogram FPCM with a standard deviation of 9.2 and a range from 0.7 to 56.3 g per kg FPCM. The average allocation factor for this study was calculated to be 89.8%. This factor is slightly higher than the default allocation factor given in the IDF guidelines (IDF, 2010) at 85.6%. Consequently, a higher proportion of emissions were allocated to milk when the calculated Australian factor (of 89.8%) was used compared with the IDF default factor.

In line with IDF (2010), economic allocation was used for the production of feed. For example, when soy beans are processed to produce soy oil the co-product 'soy meal' is also produced which is used for animal feed. The carbon footprint of the soy meal is deter- mined based on the economic value of the soy meal and the soy oil.

11: Web-based data collection software was used for the project to enable secure collection and management of confidential data, validate data entries, minimise errors due to data transfer, and create a system that can be used for future updates.

The farm questionnaire was initially developed and trialed with dairy company field officers and other agricultural experts to ensure questions were clear and only targeted relevant informa- tion. All field officers involved in on-farm data collection received software training by the research team. Farms included in the study were selected randomly by the field officers and Dairy Australia ensured that main Australian dairy regions were covered. The field officers visited each farm to collect the required data. Once data were entered into the system it was checked and validated by the research team. Where anomalies in the data were discovered, the relevant question was sent back to the field officer for confirmation and in some cases further investigation.

The percentage of farms was calculated for three categories; the proportion with a pasture based diet, the concentrate intake per cow, and the average milk yield per cow (Table 1). These results indicate that a high percentage of the farms sampled have a largely pasture based diet. Within the sample the average proportion of feed sourced from the pasture was 57%. Most farms fed between 0.5 and 1.5 tonnes of concentrate dry matter (DM) to each cow per year with the average intake of 1.3 tonnes. This correlates with the current distribution of feed systems in Australia (DA, 2010a). The average milk yield was 6250 kg per cow and annum.

There are eight dairy producing regions in Australia and the majority of dairy farming occurs in the coastal regions Victoria and New South Wales (DA, 2010b). Six regions were represented in the farm sample for this study (Table 2) with Western Australia and South Australia being the only exclusions. Tasmania, which pro- duces approximately 7% of Australia's raw milk, was under- represented with only 3 farms in the study. The regions included in the study cover approximately 90% of the Australian raw milk production. Western Australia produces approximately 4% and Southern Australia approximately 6%. 15: <header>2.5. Life cycle inventory and model description </header>

Table 3 gives an overview of the farm data. The average farm size was 261 ha ranging from 40 ha to 1795 ha. The standard deviation shows that the majority of the farms lie within a range of 226 ha. As the median and the mode were below the average, a small number of large farms tended to bias the average. The average farm herd size and total milk sold showed a similar tendency (see Table 3). How- ever, the average annual milk yield was evenly distributed around the average (median and mode was close to the average).

All impacts related to activities on the farms were calculated based on primary data. Impacts associated with fuel and electricity inputs were included using National Greenhouse Accounts Factors (NGA) data (DCCEE, 2010a, b). Impacts related to farm inputs were either from the GaBi LCA database (GaBi 5, 2012) or literature data as documented in following Section 2.5.1.
16: Purchased feed: Greenhouse gas emissions associated with the supply of forages, concentrates and other feed imported to the farm were based on data provided from the GaBi database (GaBi 5, 2012) and literature data. A list of feeds with the average content in the rations and the greenhouse gas emissions for each are shown in Table 4. All feed sources, except for soy and palm kernel products, were assumed to be supplied from within Australia. The green- house gas emission associated with direct land use change for the production of imported soy and palm kernel feeds from Brazil and Malaysia were included as described in Section 2.7.3.

The feed intake from grazing (pasture) is estimated by sub- tracting the available energy of the purchased feed and the feed grown on the farm, from the energy requirement for each of the relevant stock classes. Wastage of purchased feed was not directly accounted for. On average pasture constitutes 56% of the dry matter intake of the cows. Almost all (95%) of the concentrates were pur- chased rather than produced on-farm. In contrast to this, 75% of forages were produced on the dairy farms.

Fertilisers and soil conditioners: During the period of the study, 90% of the farms used mineral fertilisers and soil conditioners and 15% of the farms used imported organic fertilisers such as manures from other farms. The carbon footprints of mineral fertilisers were taken from the GaBi database (GaBi 2012). The datasets used for the study distinguish between the type of fertiliser (for nitrogen: ammonium nitrate, calcium ammonium nitrate, and urea; for phosphate: su- perphosphate; for potassium: potassium chloride; and for magne- sium, magnesium sulphate). Specific country where production took place was not considered due to the lack of specific data. Organic fertilisers were considered waste products and therefore greenhouse gas emissions for production were not allocated to the dairy farms. The greenhouse gas emissions from the transport and application of these organic fertilisers were included as described in Section 2.6.

Transport: Transport of inputs such as feed and fertiliser were included in the model. Within this study an average transport distance of 100 km was assumed for all inputs except for soy and palm kernel products where the specific transport distances from Malaysia and Brazil were included. excreta are applied to the soil. Nitrous oxide emissions were calculated following the IPCC (2006) Tier 2 approach using DCCEE (2010a) emission factors. Direct emissions and indirect emissions were included for: leaching; runoff and atmospheric deposition of reactive nitrogen from excreta; mineral and organic fertilisers. Direct nitrous oxide nitrogen emission factors for quantities of ni- trogen applied were 0.004 for pasture, 0.021 for irrigated cropland and 0.003 for non-irrigated cropland. The volatile fraction of fer- tilisers (ammonia and nitrogen oxides) was assumed to be 10% for synthetic fertiliser and 20% for organic fertiliser (IPCC, 2006). The IPCC (2006) emission factor of 1% was used to account for indirect nitrous oxide emissions from atmospheric deposition. The fraction of nitrogen added to soils which is lost through leaching and runoff was assumed to be 30% (IPCC, 2006). The nitrous oxide nitrogen emission factor for leached nitrogen is 0.75% (IPCC, 2006). Appli- cation rates of mineral and organic fertilisers were based on pri- mary data collected for each farm.
Following the IDF (2010) guidelines, carbon sequestration due to changes in the carbon content of soils was excluded.

24: Enteric fermentation is the digestive process during which carbohydrates are broken down in by anaerobic micro-organisms in the rumen of the animals. Methane is created as a by-product and mostly emitted through the mouth of the cow.

Methane emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated following the DCCEE (2010a) Tier 2 approach. Emissions from enteric fermentation are dependent on the feed digestibility and the amount of feed eaten. Feed intake was estimated based on the gross energy requirement of the specific animal based on the milk production, live weight and live weight gain following DCCEE (2010a). The mean feed digestibility was calculated based on the composition of the feed. Default digestibility values for different forages and concentrates were incorporated in the model (DA, 2010c). Table 5 gives an overview of the average annual methane emissions per head and stock class as calculated in this study.
25: Following IPCC (2006), methane emissions from manure man- agement depend on the amount of volatile solids excreted, the type of manure management system used and the region specific methane conversion factor (MCF). The average annual excretion of volatile solids was 1530 kg (1010-2075 kg) for dairy cows, 420- 710 kg for heifers and 165-764 kg for bulls and other cattle on the analysed farms.

The amount of manure managed in the different systems was calculated for each stock class based on the time each stock class spent in the each system. State specific MCFs were calculated for each manure management system and dairy region based on IPCC (2006) taking into account the average annual temperature. The MCF determines the degree to which the maximum methane- producing capacity of the manure is achieved. The MCF for

lagoon storage ranges from 71% for Tasmania to 76% for NSW. For solid storage the MCF ranges between 2% and 4% and for dry lot between 1% and 2%. For manure deposited onto pasture between it ranges from 1% to 2%. For manure spread daily it was between 0.5% and 0.1%. Annual methane emissions from manure management were calculated for each stock class on a farm by farm basis.

Nitrous oxide emissions from manure management depend on the amount of nitrogen excreted as faeces and urine, and the manure management system. The DCCEE (2010a) Tier 2 algorithms and emission factors were used for these calculations. The amount of nitrogen excreted was calculated based on the protein intake through feed and the protein required for growth, reproduction, and milk production. The crude protein intake was calculated for each stock class based on the composition of the feed ration and average values for crude protein content of individual feeds. Average crude protein content values were taken from DA (2010c). For pasture an average crude protein content of 16.5% was assumed (DA, 2010c). Nitrogen excretion was calculated for faeces and urine excretion. Average annual excretion rates for dairy cows were 30 kg of nitrogen as faeces and 89 kg of nitrogen as urine.

The fraction of urine and faeces going to each manure manage- ment system was calculated based on the percentage of time each stock class spends at various locations on the farm (i.e., grazing pastures, at the dairy or on a feed-pad area). Liquid manure can be collected in a sump and spread daily onto pastures/crops or it is stored in an anaerobic lagoon and spread at a later date. Solid manure, which is separated from the liquid manure through the use of a separation system, and other solid waste from areas such as feed- pads is can also be spread daily or stored and spread at a later time.

On average, 88% of the manure produced annually was voided directly onto pastures and only 12% collected while the herd was housed or in the milking shed. Of the manure collected, 7% was stored as liquid manure, 3% was stored as solid manure and 2% was spread daily. If a solid-liquid separation system for liquid manure was used it was assumed that 20% of volatile solids were separated, stored as solid manure and spread at a later date (Birchall, Dillon, Dillon, & Wrigley, 2008).
26: Following IPCC (2006), methane emissions from manure man- agement depend on the amount of volatile solids excreted, the type of manure management system used and the region specific methane conversion factor (MCF). The average annual excretion of volatile solids was 1530 kg (1010-2075 kg) for dairy cows, 420- 710 kg for heifers and 165-764 kg for bulls and other cattle on the analysed farms.

The amount of manure managed in the different systems was calculated for each stock class based on the time each stock class spent in the each system. State specific MCFs were calculated for each manure management system and dairy region based on IPCC (2006) taking into account the average annual temperature. The MCF determines the degree to which the maximum methane- producing capacity of the manure is achieved. The MCF for

lagoon storage ranges from 71% for Tasmania to 76% for NSW. For solid storage the MCF ranges between 2% and 4% and for dry lot between 1% and 2%. 29: The IDF (2010) guidelines require the inclusion of GHG emis- sions arising from direct land use change (dLUC) if it occurred after 1990. Within this study it was assumed that all agricultural area in Australia has been under cultivation before 1990. Thus the assumption was that land use on the dairy farms has not changed and was therefore not associated with direct land use change.

For some of the farms, the purchased grain and concentrate mixes contain soy and palm kernel products. In this study we assumed that soy and palm kernel products were imported from Brazil and Malaysia and included emissions from land use change. This assumption is realistic for palm kernel products, while for soy it is a worst-case scenario as some of the soy could have been from areas where emissions from land use change are lower (e.g., USA or Australia). Land use change was modelled according to IPCC (2007), which is in line with IDF (2010). The countries from which soy is sourced changes from year to year. In 2007 more than half of the Australian imports came from Brazil, while in 2009 two thirds came from Argentina and the United States (USDA, 2010). If the assumption was made that soy products were not associated with directly land use change, this would have reduced the average carbon footprint by an insignificant amount (1%).

increase the representativeness of the results as larger producers have a greater effect on the overall mean.

The average carbon footprint of Australian milk was found to be 1.11 CO2 e kg FPCM based on the farms sampled. In this study, 89.8% of the GHG emissions of the farms were allocated to the milk and 10.2% to the meat. If the IDF (2010) default milk-meat allo- cation factor of 85.6% was used in this study, the average carbon footprint result would be 1.06 kg CO2 e kg FPCM. The standard deviation for the sample was 0.22 kg CO2 e kg FPCM or 19%. The median of 1.08 kg CO2 e kg- FPCM is slightly lower than the average indicating that the sample is slightly biased towards a fewer large milk producers.

Enteric fermentation contributed the largest share (57%) of the carbon footprint of the milk. The majority of these emissions (80%) were emitted by the milkers, 12% by all heifers, 1% by bulls and 2% by other cattle (i.e., suckler cows, female non lactating COWS or COWS for beef production). Emissions from manure deposited directly onto pastures during grazing contributed the second largest share of the carbon footprint with 9% from N20 and 1% from CH4. The application of stored manure (to arable land and pastures) contributed 8% overall with 2% from N20 and 6% from CH4. Concentrate feed production contributed a significant share of 8%. Electricity used (for pumps, conveyor belts, mills, etc.), fuels (for generators, tractors and other engines) and auxiliary materials, all together produced 8% of the carbon footprint. The production of mineral fertiliser and lime contributed 4% and 1% respectively. The application of organic and mineral nitrogen fertilisers added a relatively small share (3%) to the carbon footprint. The area with the lowest contribution to the carbon footprint was the off-site forage production (1%).

Fig. 2 shows the weighted average carbon footprint and the various sources that contribute to it. The boxplot shows the dis- tribution and variation of the results. The vertical line represents the distribution of 10%-90% of the sample; the box represents the lower and upper quartile and the horizontal line the weighted average. The lower quartile for the average carbon footprint is 0.98 kg CO2 e kg` FPCM. The upper quartile is 1.22 kg CO2 e kg-1 FPCM. The carbon footprint of 80% of the sample is within a range of 0.90-1.39 kg CO2 e kg) FPCM. A slightly higher variation can be observed for enteric fermentation, fertiliser (production and use), and manure management.

The carbon footprint was influenced by a range of variables with low correlation when looking at the whole sample. However, a statistically significant correlation (p < 0.05) was observed be- tween the carbon footprint and the average dry matter intake per kg FPCM produced (feed conversion efficiency, R2 0.36, p < 0.05), the average annual milk yield 0.05) and the concentrate intake 0.14, p 0.05).

Although only the milkers produce the milk, the other stock classes (heifers, bulls, etc.) are all part of the dairy farm and contribute to the carbon footprint. Fig. 3 shows the enteric fermentation for the different stock classes and that the milkers and the heifers are responsible for the majority of the enteric emissions per kilogram of FPCM produced. The highest variation can be observed for the stock class milkers (weighted average 0.49 kg CO2 e kg™ FPCM; standard deviation (s.d.) = 0.08) and heifers (weighted average 0.11 kg CO2 e kg FPCM; s.d. = 0.04).

Fig. 4 shows the relationship between how the manure is distributed (column 1) and the greenhouse gas emissions (column 2). On average 88% of manure is excreted during grazing onto pastures but it is only associated with 58% of total GHG emissions from with manure. In contrast to this, only 3% of the manure is stored in anaerobic lagoons but this manure handling method is the second largest contributor to GHG emissions (32%) associated with manure. Manure stored as solid manure represented 7% of
Fig. 2. Average carbon footprint and absolute contribution of the different sources.
the total amount of manure but it was only associated with 3% of GHG emissions.

This project was one of the first carbon footprint projects to assess dairy farming on a country level and follow the IDF (2010) guidelines. When comparing the results of this study with other studies it is important to consider the differences in the method- ologies used such as the allocation rules, system boundaries, emission factors and characterisation factors. Due to the high contribution of enteric methane emissions to the carbon footprint of milk the characterisation factor used for methane (21 or 25 kg CO2 e kg- ) can have a substantial impact on the final results. Also, the results for this study are based on data for the 2009/2010 period and therefore do not represent a long term average.

The International Dairy Federation published an extensive literature review in 2009 (IDF, 2009) considering 60 different publications, all referring to western industrialised agricultural systems (22 studies from Scandinavia, 19 from the EU, 6 from Australia and New Zealand, 4 other countries and 9 global studies). From these studies the average carbon footprint for 1 kg of milk at the farm gate was calculated to be 1 kg CO2 e with a range from 0.4 to 1.81 (IDF, 2009). Despite the methodological differences explained above, the results of this study are similar to the average calculated by the IDF.
Fig. 3. Methane enteric fermentation: absolute contribution of the stock classes.

There have been other carbon footprint studies conducted within the Australian dairy sector, although the goal, scope and methodologies of these studies vary. Dairy Australia previously conducted a LCA of the major Australian dairy products in 2002 (Lundie et al., 2002) using primary data from eight farms and secondary data from industry surveys. The functional unit of that study was 1 kg of raw milk (as opposed to FPCM) and there was no allocation between the production of milk and meat. An overview of other Australian studies and their scope and key methodological considerations is shown in Table 6.

The methodology applied in the studies Christie, Gourley, Rawnsley, Eckard, and Awty (2012) and Christie, Rawnsley, and Eckard (2011) was based on the DGAS (Dairy GHG Abatement Strategies) calculator (see Table 6). The calculation approach and

(2012)|Dairy Australia 2012 (this study) Geographical reference|Australia|Tasmania|Australia|Australia Scope of the study|LCA|DGAS|DGAS|LCA Carbon footprint (kg CO2 e milk)|1.045 kg|1.04|1.04|1.11 kg Studied systems, data source|8 surveyed farms and literature|Primary data 60 farms with complete inventory|Primary data 41 farms with complete inventory|Primary data, 140 farms with complete inventory Functional unit|1 kg raw milk|1 kg FPCM|1 kg FPCM|1 kg FPCM GWP characterisation factors|IPCC (1995)|IPCC (1995)|IPCC (1995)|IPCC (2007) Consideration of land use change Allocation|No No allocation|No No allocation|No No allocation|Yes, palm kernel and soybean products Individual farm calculation of milk to meat allocation values, according to IDF method.

emission factors used to quantify emissions from on farm activities are very similar to those adopted for this study; however, the characterisation factors were different: DGAS uses 21 for methane and 310 for nitrous oxide compared with 25 for methane and 289 for nitrous oxide in this study. Emissions from upstream activities (production of farm inputs) were also considered in greater detail in this study and different sources of emission factors were used.

To validate the model used in this study results were compared with the DGAS calculator using primary data from a sub-sample of six farms. For five of the six farms, the difference in results was less than 10% which can be explained by the difference in char- acterisation factors. One farm was an exception with an estimated 16% higher carbon footprint of milk production using the approach from this study compared to the amended DGAS approach. Differences were tracked back to methodological dif- ferences in the system boundaries as the DGAS ignores the impact of co-products used in feeds such as soy meal and palm kernel expeller. Differences were also attributed to the use of different emission factors for the production of farm inputs such as mineral fertiliser, feed and energy. The comparison Indicated that the calculation methods used to quantify on farm emissions such as enteric fermentation and manure management in this project are in line with DGAS and the Australian National GHG Inventory Methodology (DCCEE, 2010a,b).

Several methods to reduce the carbon footprint were identified that are also supported in the published literature. The enteric methane emissions from the milkers, which were identified as the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions in this study, could be effectively reduced by increasing the feed conversion efficiency (Ellis, 2010; Flachowsky & Brade, 2007; Gerber, Vellinga, Opio, & Steinfeld, 2011; Nguyen et al., 2013; Thoma et al., 2012). A high correlation (R2 0.80, < 0.05) between enteric CH4 emissions and feed conversion efficiency was observed. Other measures such as an increase in the average milk yield had a low correlation to enteric methane emissions. Further research is required to analyse reduction potentials of relevant herd management parameters such as mortality rate, age of first calving, number of lactations and replacement rate.

Methane emissions from manure stored in anaerobic lagoons can potentially be reduced by implementing solid-liquid separa- tion systems (Birchall et al., 2008). Solid-liquid separation systems were used by one quarter of the farms in this study indicating that there is some room for improvement. Lagoons can also be covered to capture the methane so it can be used or flared. The use of manure digestion systems (Baylis & Paulson, 2011) could also reduce the carbon footprint.

One methodological issue further considered in this study is the allocation between milk and meat (livestock) at the farm gate. The IDF (2010) provides both a default value and a regression equation to calculate a precise allocation factor for each individual farm. In
this study, it was decided to follow the regression approach due to its greater accuracy.

The average carbon footprint of Australian milk was found to be 1.11 CO2 e kg FPCM based on the farms sampled. In this study, 89.8% of the GHG emissions of the farms were allocated to the milk and 10.2% to the meat. If the IDF (2010) default milk-meat allo- cation factor of 85.6% was used in this study, the average carbon footprint result would be 1.06 kg CO2 e kg FPCM. The standard deviation for the sample was 0.22 kg CO2 e kg FPCM or 19%. The median of 1.08 kg CO2 e kg- FPCM is slightly lower than the average indicating that the sample is slightly biased towards a fewer large milk producers.

Enteric fermentation contributed the largest share (57%) of the carbon footprint of the milk. The majority of these emissions (80%) were emitted by the milkers, 12% by all heifers, 1% by bulls and 2% by other cattle (i.e., suckler cows, female non lactating COWS or COWS for beef production). Emissions from manure deposited directly onto pastures during grazing contributed the second largest share of the carbon footprint with 9% from N20 and 1% from CH4. The application of stored manure (to arable land and pastures) contributed 8% overall with 2% from N20 and 6% from CH4. Concentrate feed production contributed a significant share of 8%. Electricity used (for pumps, conveyor belts, mills, etc.), fuels (for generators, tractors and other engines) and auxiliary materials, all together produced 8% of the carbon footprint. The production of mineral fertiliser and lime contributed 4% and 1% respectively. The application of organic and mineral nitrogen fertilisers added a relatively small share (3%) to the carbon footprint. The area with the lowest contribution to the carbon footprint was the off-site forage production (1%).

Fig. 2 shows the weighted average carbon footprint and the various sources that contribute to it. The boxplot shows the dis- tribution and variation of the results. The vertical line represents the distribution of 10%-90% of the sample; the box represents the lower and upper quartile and the horizontal line the weighted average. The lower quartile for the average carbon footprint is 0.98 kg CO2 e kg` FPCM. The upper quartile is 1.22 kg CO2 e kg-1 FPCM. The carbon footprint of 80% of the sample is within a range of 0.90-1.39 kg CO2 e kg) FPCM. A slightly higher variation can be observed for enteric fermentation, fertiliser (production and use), and manure management.

The carbon footprint was influenced by a range of variables with low correlation when looking at the whole sample. However, a statistically significant correlation (p < 0.05) was observed be- tween the carbon footprint and the average dry matter intake per kg FPCM produced (feed conversion efficiency, R2 0.36, p < 0.05), the average annual milk yield 0.05) and the concentrate intake 0.14, p 0.05).

Although only the milkers produce the milk, the other stock classes (heifers, bulls, etc.) are all part of the dairy farm and contribute to the carbon footprint. Fig. 3 shows the enteric fermentation for the different stock classes and that the milkers and the heifers are responsible for the majority of the enteric emissions per kilogram of FPCM produced. The highest variation can be observed for the stock class milkers (weighted average 0.49 kg CO2 e kg™ FPCM; standard deviation (s.d.) = 0.08) and heifers (weighted average 0.11 kg CO2 e kg FPCM; s.d. = 0.04).

Fig. 4 shows the relationship between how the manure is distributed (column 1) and the greenhouse gas emissions (column 2). On average 88% of manure is excreted during grazing onto pastures but it is only associated with 58% of total GHG emissions from with manure. In contrast to this, only 3% of the manure is stored in anaerobic lagoons but this manure handling method is the second largest contributor to GHG emissions (32%) associated with manure. Manure stored as solid manure represented 7% of
Fig. 2. Average carbon footprint and absolute contribution of the different sources.
the total amount of manure but it was only associated with 3% of GHG emissions.

This project was one of the first carbon footprint projects to assess dairy farming on a country level and follow the IDF (2010) guidelines. When comparing the results of this study with other studies it is important to consider the differences in the method- ologies used such as the allocation rules, system boundaries, emission factors and characterisation factors. Due to the high contribution of enteric methane emissions to the carbon footprint of milk the characterisation factor used for methane (21 or 25 kg CO2 e kg- ) can have a substantial impact on the final results. Also, the results for this study are based on data for the 2009/2010 period and therefore do not represent a long term average.

The International Dairy Federation published an extensive literature review in 2009 (IDF, 2009) considering 60 different publications, all referring to western industrialised agricultural systems (22 studies from Scandinavia, 19 from the EU, 6 from Australia and New Zealand, 4 other countries and 9 global studies). From these studies the average carbon footprint for 1 kg of milk at the farm gate was calculated to be 1 kg CO2 e with a range from 0.4 to 1.81 (IDF, 2009). Despite the methodological differences explained above, the results of this study are similar to the average calculated by the IDF.

There have been other carbon footprint studies conducted within the Australian dairy sector, although the goal, scope and methodologies of these studies vary. Dairy Australia previously conducted a LCA of the major Australian dairy products in 2002 (Lundie et al., 2002) using primary data from eight farms and secondary data from industry surveys. The functional unit of that study was 1 kg of raw milk (as opposed to FPCM) and there was no allocation between the production of milk and meat. An overview of other Australian studies and their scope and key methodological considerations is shown in Table 6.

The methodology applied in the studies Christie, Gourley, Rawnsley, Eckard, and Awty (2012) and Christie, Rawnsley, and Eckard (2011) was based on the DGAS (Dairy GHG Abatement Strategies) calculator (see Table 6). The calculation approach and

(2012)|Dairy Australia 2012 (this study) Geographical reference|Australia|Tasmania|Australia|Australia Scope of the study|LCA|DGAS|DGAS|LCA Carbon footprint (kg CO2 e milk)|1.045 kg|1.04|1.04|1.11 kg Studied systems, data source|8 surveyed farms and literature|Primary data 60 farms with complete inventory|Primary data 41 farms with complete inventory|Primary data, 140 farms with complete inventory Functional unit|1 kg raw milk|1 kg FPCM|1 kg FPCM|1 kg FPCM GWP characterisation factors|IPCC (1995)|IPCC (1995)|IPCC (1995)|IPCC (2007) Consideration of land use change Allocation|No No allocation|No No allocation|No No allocation|Yes, palm kernel and soybean products Individual farm calculation of milk to meat allocation values, according to IDF method.

emission factors used to quantify emissions from on farm activities are very similar to those adopted for this study; however, the characterisation factors were different: DGAS uses 21 for methane and 310 for nitrous oxide compared with 25 for methane and 289 for nitrous oxide in this study. Emissions from upstream activities (production of farm inputs) were also considered in greater detail in this study and different sources of emission factors were used.

To validate the model used in this study results were compared with the DGAS calculator using primary data from a sub-sample of six farms. For five of the six farms, the difference in results was less than 10% which can be explained by the difference in char- acterisation factors. One farm was an exception with an estimated 16% higher carbon footprint of milk production using the approach from this study compared to the amended DGAS approach. Differences were tracked back to methodological dif- ferences in the system boundaries as the DGAS ignores the impact of co-products used in feeds such as soy meal and palm kernel expeller. Differences were also attributed to the use of different emission factors for the production of farm inputs such as mineral fertiliser, feed and energy. The comparison Indicated that the calculation methods used to quantify on farm emissions such as enteric fermentation and manure management in this project are in line with DGAS and the Australian National GHG Inventory Methodology (DCCEE, 2010a,b).

Several methods to reduce the carbon footprint were identified that are also supported in the published literature. The enteric methane emissions from the milkers, which were identified as the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions in this study, could be effectively reduced by increasing the feed conversion efficiency (Ellis, 2010; Flachowsky & Brade, 2007; Gerber, Vellinga, Opio, & Steinfeld, 2011; Nguyen et al., 2013; Thoma et al., 2012). A high correlation (R2 0.80, < 0.05) between enteric CH4 emissions and feed conversion efficiency was observed. Other measures such as an increase in the average milk yield had a low correlation to enteric methane emissions. Further research is required to analyse reduction potentials of relevant herd management parameters such as mortality rate, age of first calving, number of lactations and replacement rate.

Methane emissions from manure stored in anaerobic lagoons can potentially be reduced by implementing solid-liquid separa- tion systems (Birchall et al., 2008). Solid-liquid separation systems were used by one quarter of the farms in this study indicating that there is some room for improvement. Lagoons can also be covered to capture the methane so it can be used or flared. The use of manure digestion systems (Baylis & Paulson, 2011) could also reduce the carbon footprint.

One methodological issue further considered in this study is the allocation between milk and meat (livestock) at the farm gate. The IDF (2010) provides both a default value and a regression equation to calculate a precise allocation factor for each individual farm. In
this study, it was decided to follow the regression approach due to its greater accuracy.

An overview of other Australian studies and their scope and key methodological considerations is shown in Table 6.

The methodology applied in the studies Christie, Gourley, Rawnsley, Eckard, and Awty (2012) and Christie, Rawnsley, and Eckard (2011) was based on the DGAS (Dairy GHG Abatement Strategies) calculator (see Table 6). The calculation approach and

Emissions from upstream activities (production of farm inputs) were also considered in greater detail in this study and different sources of emission factors were used.

To validate the model used in this study results were compared with the DGAS calculator using primary data from a sub-sample of six farms. For five of the six farms, the difference in results was less than 10% which can be explained by the difference in char- acterisation factors. One farm was an exception with an estimated 16% higher carbon footprint of milk production using the approach from this study compared to the amended DGAS approach. Differences were tracked back to methodological dif- ferences in the system boundaries as the DGAS ignores the impact of co-products used in feeds such as soy meal and palm kernel expeller. Differences were also attributed to the use of different emission factors for the production of farm inputs such as mineral fertiliser, feed and energy. The comparison Indicated that the calculation methods used to quantify on farm emissions such as enteric fermentation and manure management in this project are in line with DGAS and the Australian National GHG Inventory Methodology (DCCEE, 2010a,b).

Several methods to reduce the carbon footprint were identified that are also supported in the published literature. The enteric methane emissions from the milkers, which were identified as the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions in this study, could be effectively reduced by increasing the feed conversion efficiency (Ellis, 2010; Flachowsky & Brade, 2007; Gerber, Vellinga, Opio, & Steinfeld, 2011; Nguyen et al., 2013; Thoma et al., 2012). A high correlation (R2 0.80, < 0.05) between enteric CH4 emissions and feed conversion efficiency was observed. Other measures such as an increase in the average milk yield had a low correlation to enteric methane emissions. Further research is required to analyse reduction potentials of relevant herd management parameters such as mortality rate, age of first calving, number of lactations and replacement rate.

Methane emissions from manure stored in anaerobic lagoons can potentially be reduced by implementing solid-liquid separa- tion systems (Birchall et al., 2008). Solid-liquid separation systems were used by one quarter of the farms in this study indicating that there is some room for improvement. Lagoons can also be covered to capture the methane so it can be used or flared. The use of manure digestion systems (Baylis & Paulson, 2011) could also reduce the carbon footprint.

One methodological issue further considered in this study is the allocation between milk and meat (livestock) at the farm gate. The IDF (2010) provides both a default value and a regression equation to calculate a precise allocation factor for each individual farm. In
this study, it was decided to follow the regression approach due to its greater accuracy.

The standard deviation for the sample was 0.22 kg CO2 e kg FPCM or 19%. The median of 1.08 kg CO2 e kg- FPCM is slightly lower than the average indicating that the sample is slightly biased towards a fewer large milk producers.

Enteric fermentation contributed the largest share (57%) of the carbon footprint of the milk. The production of mineral fertiliser and lime contributed 4% and 1% respectively. The application of organic and mineral nitrogen fertilisers added a relatively small share (3%) to the carbon footprint. The area with the lowest contribution to the carbon footprint was the off-site forage production (1%).

To validate the model used in this study results were compared with the DGAS calculator using primary data from a sub-sample of six farms. For five of the six farms, the difference in results was less than 10% which can be explained by the difference in char- acterisation factors. One farm was an exception with an estimated 16% higher carbon footprint of milk production using the approach from this study compared to the amended DGAS approach. Differences were tracked back to methodological dif- ferences in the system boundaries as the DGAS ignores the impact of co-products used in feeds such as soy meal and palm kernel expeller. Differences were also attributed to the use of different emission factors for the production of farm inputs such as mineral fertiliser, feed and energy. The comparison Indicated that the calculation methods used to quantify on farm emissions such as enteric fermentation and manure management in this project are in line with DGAS and the Australian National GHG Inventory Methodology (DCCEE, 2010a,b).

Several methods to reduce the carbon footprint were identified that are also supported in the published literature. The enteric methane emissions from the milkers, which were identified as the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions in this study, could be effectively reduced by increasing the feed conversion efficiency (Ellis, 2010; Flachowsky & Brade, 2007; Gerber, Vellinga, Opio, & Steinfeld, 2011; Nguyen et al., 2013; Thoma et al., 2012). A high correlation (R2 0.80, < 0.05) between enteric CH4 emissions and feed conversion efficiency was observed. Other measures such as an increase in the average milk yield had a low correlation to enteric methane emissions. Further research is required to analyse reduction potentials of relevant herd management parameters such as mortality rate, age of first calving, number of lactations and replacement rate.

Methane emissions from manure stored in anaerobic lagoons can potentially be reduced by implementing solid-liquid separa- tion systems (Birchall et al., 2008). Solid-liquid separation systems were used by one quarter of the farms in this study indicating that there is some room for improvement. Lagoons can also be covered to capture the methane so it can be used or flared. The use of manure digestion systems (Baylis & Paulson, 2011) could also reduce the carbon footprint.

One methodological issue further considered in this study is the allocation between milk and meat (livestock) at the farm gate. The IDF (2010) provides both a default value and a regression equation to calculate a precise allocation factor for each individual farm. In
this study, it was decided to follow the regression approach due to its greater accuracy.

The average carbon footprint of Australian milk was found to be 1.11 CO2 e kg FPCM based on the farms sampled. In this study, 89.8% of the GHG emissions of the farms were allocated to the milk and 10.2% to the meat. If the IDF (2010) default milk-meat allo- cation factor of 85.6% was used in this study, the average carbon footprint result would be 1.06 kg CO2 e kg FPCM. The standard deviation for the sample was 0.22 kg CO2 e kg FPCM or 19%. The median of 1.08 kg CO2 e kg- FPCM is slightly lower than the average indicating that the sample is slightly biased towards a fewer large milk producers.

Enteric fermentation contributed the largest share (57%) of the carbon footprint of the milk. The majority of these emissions (80%) were emitted by the milkers, 12% by all heifers, 1% by bulls and 2% by other cattle (i.e., suckler cows, female non lactating COWS or COWS for beef production). Emissions from manure deposited directly onto pastures during grazing contributed the second largest share of the carbon footprint with 9% from N20 and 1% from CH4. The application of stored manure (to arable land and pastures) contributed 8% overall with 2% from N20 and 6% from CH4. Concentrate feed production contributed a significant share of 8%. Electricity used (for pumps, conveyor belts, mills, etc.), fuels (for generators, tractors and other engines) and auxiliary materials, all together produced 8% of the carbon footprint. The production of mineral fertiliser and lime contributed 4% and 1% respectively. The application of organic and mineral nitrogen fertilisers added a relatively small share (3%) to the carbon footprint. The area with the lowest contribution to the carbon footprint was the off-site forage production (1%).

Fig. 2 shows the weighted average carbon footprint and the various sources that contribute to it. The boxplot shows the dis- tribution and variation of the results. The vertical line represents the distribution of 10%-90% of the sample; the box represents the lower and upper quartile and the horizontal line the weighted average. The lower quartile for the average carbon footprint is 0.98 kg CO2 e kg` FPCM. The upper quartile is 1.22 kg CO2 e kg-1 FPCM. The carbon footprint of 80% of the sample is within a range of 0.90-1.39 kg CO2 e kg) FPCM. A slightly higher variation can be observed for enteric fermentation, fertiliser (production and use), and manure management.

The carbon footprint was influenced by a range of variables with low correlation when looking at the whole sample. However, a statistically significant correlation (p < 0.05) was observed be- tween the carbon footprint and the average dry matter intake per kg FPCM produced (feed conversion efficiency, R2 0.36, p < 0.05), the average annual milk yield 0.05) and the concentrate intake 0.14, p 0.05).

Although only the milkers produce the milk, the other stock classes (heifers, bulls, etc.) are all part of the dairy farm and contribute to the carbon footprint. Fig. 3 shows the enteric fermentation for the different stock classes and that the milkers and the heifers are responsible for the majority of the enteric emissions per kilogram of FPCM produced. The highest variation can be observed for the stock class milkers (weighted average 0.49 kg CO2 e kg™ FPCM; standard deviation (s.d.) = 0.08) and heifers (weighted average 0.11 kg CO2 e kg FPCM; s.d. = 0.04).

Fig. 4 shows the relationship between how the manure is distributed (column 1) and the greenhouse gas emissions (column 2). On average 88% of manure is excreted during grazing onto pastures but it is only associated with 58% of total GHG emissions from with manure. In contrast to this, only 3% of the manure is stored in anaerobic lagoons but this manure handling method is the second largest contributor to GHG emissions (32%) associated with manure. Manure stored as solid manure represented 7% of

the total amount of manure but it was only associated with 3% of GHG emissions.

emission factors used to quantify emissions from on farm activities are very similar to those adopted for this study; however, the characterisation factors were different: DGAS uses 21 for methane and 310 for nitrous oxide compared with 25 for methane and 289 for nitrous oxide in this study. Emissions from upstream activities (production of farm inputs) were also considered in greater detail in this study and different sources of emission factors were used.

To validate the model used in this study results were compared with the DGAS calculator using primary data from a sub-sample of six farms. For five of the six farms, the difference in results was less than 10% which can be explained by the difference in char- acterisation factors. One farm was an exception with an estimated 16% higher carbon footprint of milk production using the approach from this study compared to the amended DGAS approach. Differences were tracked back to methodological dif- ferences in the system boundaries as the DGAS ignores the impact of co-products used in feeds such as soy meal and palm kernel expeller. Differences were also attributed to the use of different emission factors for the production of farm inputs such as mineral fertiliser, feed and energy. The comparison Indicated that the calculation methods used to quantify on farm emissions such as enteric fermentation and manure management in this project are in line with DGAS and the Australian National GHG Inventory Methodology (DCCEE, 2010a,b).

Several methods to reduce the carbon footprint were identified that are also supported in the published literature. The enteric methane emissions from the milkers, which were identified as the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions in this study, could be effectively reduced by increasing the feed conversion efficiency (Ellis, 2010; Flachowsky & Brade, 2007; Gerber, Vellinga, Opio, & Steinfeld, 2011; Nguyen et al., 2013; Thoma et al., 2012). A high correlation (R2 0.80, < 0.05) between enteric CH4 emissions and feed conversion efficiency was observed. Other measures such as an increase in the average milk yield had a low correlation to enteric methane emissions. Further research is required to analyse reduction potentials of relevant herd management parameters such as mortality rate, age of first calving, number of lactations and replacement rate.

Methane emissions from manure stored in anaerobic lagoons can potentially be reduced by implementing solid-liquid separa- tion systems (Birchall et al., 2008). Solid-liquid separation systems were used by one quarter of the farms in this study indicating that there is some room for improvement. Lagoons can also be covered to capture the methane so it can be used or flared. The use of manure digestion systems (Baylis & Paulson, 2011) could also reduce the carbon footprint.

One methodological issue further considered in this study is the allocation between milk and meat (livestock) at the farm gate. The IDF (2010) provides both a default value and a regression equation to calculate a precise allocation factor for each individual farm. In
this study, it was decided to follow the regression approach due to its greater accuracy.
43: Allocation between milk and meat in carbon footprint and LCA studies is an important methodological issue that can affect the final result. Within this study the method according the IDF (2010) was followed based on an equation that accounts for the milk and meat output of the specific farms and the specific energy re- quirements of the stock classes to produce meat and milk. Some of the farms analysed rear calves that are sold to other farms or culled for meat production and not used to replace the milking herd. It is questionable to what extent the IDF (2010) allocation approach sufficiently accounts for cattle reared on the farms that are not directly related to milk production. This question requires further research and could not be fully answered within this study. Despite these minor issues, the IDF (2010) methodology was found to be straightforward to apply and is important to produce results that are representative and can be compared.
